title: Enforce TLS for PostgreSQL connections
description: Implement mandatory TLS encryption for all PostgreSQL connections to the `sigma1-postgres` cluster. This involves configuring the CloudNative-PG Cluster CR to enforce `hostssl` and include `spec.certificates`, updating the `CNPG_SIGMA1_URL` in the `sigma1-infra-endpoints` ConfigMap to use `sslmode=verify-full`, and ensuring all services mount the CNPG-generated CA certificate for validation.
subtasks: Update `sigma1-postgres` Cluster CR (subtask 1002) to include `spec.postgresql.pg_hba` entries enforcing `hostssl` for all users and `spec.certificates` for TLS.
Generate and configure necessary TLS certificates for the PostgreSQL cluster via CloudNative-PG.
Modify `CNPG_SIGMA1_URL` in the `sigma1-infra-endpoints` ConfigMap (subtask 1007) to append `?sslmode=verify-full` to the connection string.
Update all service deployments (equipment-catalog, RMS, finance, customer-vetting, social-engine, Morgan) to mount the CNPG-generated CA certificate into their pods.
Update application code in all services to trust the mounted CA certificate when establishing PostgreSQL connections.
Conduct a penetration test to verify that unencrypted connections to PostgreSQL are rejected.
acceptance_criteria: All connections to `sigma1-postgres` from within the cluster are encrypted with TLS.
Services fail to connect if `sslmode=verify-full` is not used or if the CA certificate is invalid/missing.
The `sigma1-postgres` Cluster CR reflects TLS enforcement configuration.
The `sigma1-infra-endpoints` ConfigMap contains the updated `CNPG_SIGMA1_URL` with `sslmode=verify-full`.
All service pods successfully connect to PostgreSQL using TLS.

title: Enable authentication for Valkey
description: Enable authentication for the Valkey instance by configuring `requirepass` on the Valkey CR and storing the password in a dedicated Kubernetes Secret. All services connecting to Valkey must be updated to include this password in their connection strings.
subtasks: Update the Valkey CR (subtask 1004) to enable `requirepass` and generate a strong password.
Create a new Kubernetes Secret to store the Valkey password.
Update the Valkey CR to reference the new password Secret.
Modify the `VALKEY_SIGMA1_URL` in the `sigma1-infra-endpoints` ConfigMap (subtask 1007) to include a placeholder for the password (e.g., `redis://:$(VALKEY_PASSWORD)@...`).
Update all service deployments (equipment-catalog, RMS, finance, customer-vetting, social-engine) to mount the Valkey password Secret and inject it as an environment variable.
Update application code in all services to use the Valkey password in their connection strings.
Conduct a penetration test to verify that unauthenticated connections to Valkey are rejected.
acceptance_criteria: Valkey requires a password for all connections.
All services successfully connect to Valkey using the configured password.
The Valkey password is stored in a Kubernetes Secret, not in a ConfigMap or application code.
Unauthenticated connections to Valkey are rejected.

title: Implement asymmetric JWT signing and validation
description: Transition from a single symmetric `JWT_SECRET` to asymmetric JWT signing (e.g., RS256 or ES256) to enhance security. This requires a dedicated identity-issuing service or a JWKS endpoint for public key distribution, and all consuming services must validate tokens using the public key, along with enforcing `aud` and `iss` claims and implementing a key rotation mechanism.
subtasks: Design and implement a dedicated identity-issuing service or a JWKS endpoint to provide public keys for JWT validation.
Generate asymmetric key pairs (private for signing, public for verification). Store the private key securely.
Update the `sigma1-jwt-secret` (subtask 1005) to store the private key for the issuing service and distribute public keys to consuming services.
Modify JWT middleware in all services (equipment-catalog 2003, finance 4010, customer-vetting 5007, social-engine 6014, Morgan 7001) to use the public key for validation.
Implement `aud` (audience) and `iss` (issuer) claim validation in all JWT-consuming services.
Create a key rotation runbook for asymmetric JWT keys, integrating with the existing secret rotation CronJob (subtask 10012).
Conduct a security audit to ensure only public keys are used for validation in consuming services and that tokens without valid `aud`/`iss` claims are rejected.
acceptance_criteria: JWTs are signed using an asymmetric algorithm (e.g., RS256).
Consuming services validate JWTs using only the public key from a trusted source (JWKS endpoint or identity service).
All services enforce `aud` and `iss` claim validation.
A documented key rotation process for asymmetric JWT keys exists.
Compromise of one service does not compromise the JWT signing key for all other services.

title: Standardize internal/GDPR endpoint authentication
description: Standardize the authentication mechanism for all internal and GDPR-related endpoints across all services. This involves adopting a consistent, strong authentication method (e.g., short-lived signed internal service tokens or mTLS client certificate verification) and removing reliance on weak, ad-hoc headers.
subtasks: Choose a standardized internal authentication mechanism: either short-lived signed internal service tokens (e.g., JWT with 'internal' audience) or mTLS client certificate verification.
If using internal service tokens: Implement a token issuance and validation library/middleware for all services.
If using mTLS: Configure mTLS for inter-service communication, ensuring client certificates are validated.

Remove ad-hoc `X-Internal-Request` or `X-Internal-Key` header checks from all internal/GDPR endpoints (equipment-catalog 2010, RMS 3011, finance 4012, customer-vetting 5007, social-engine 6014, Morgan 7004).
Update all internal API calls (e.g., Morgan's GDPR tools 7004) to use the new standardized authentication method.
Document the chosen internal authentication approach and enforce it consistently across all five services.
Conduct an internal penetration test to verify that unauthorized access to `/internal/*` endpoints is prevented without the standardized authentication.
acceptance_criteria: All `/internal/*` and GDPR endpoints use a standardized, strong authentication mechanism.
Ad-hoc `X-Internal-Request` or `X-Internal-Key` headers are no longer used for authentication.
Unauthorized access to internal endpoints is consistently rejected.
The chosen authentication method is documented and enforced.

title: Mandate secure Stripe webhook signature validation
description: Ensure Stripe webhook signature validation in the Finance service is implemented securely, mandating the use of the `stripe-rust` crate's built-in `webhook::Webhook::construct_event()` method to prevent timing attacks and ensure raw body preservation.
subtasks: Update the Stripe webhook endpoint implementation (subtask 4005) in the Finance service to exclusively use `stripe-rust` crate's `webhook::Webhook::construct_event()` for signature validation.
Implement an Axum extractor that captures the raw request body bytes before any deserialization, ensuring it is available for `construct_event()`.
Add an explicit integration test case that verifies a tampered Stripe-Signature header is correctly rejected by the `construct_event()` method.
Review the Finance service's webhook handler to confirm no naive string comparison or improper body handling is present.
acceptance_criteria: Stripe webhook signature validation uses `stripe-rust::webhook::Webhook::construct_event()`.
The raw request body is preserved and passed to the validation function.
Integration tests confirm rejection of tampered signatures.
No timing attacks are possible due to constant-time comparison.

title: Harden Container Image Supply Chain
description: Improve the security of the container image supply chain by pinning all image tags to specific digests, implementing a container image scanning step in the CI/CD pipeline, and blocking deployments with critical vulnerabilities.
subtasks: Update the Signal-CLI deployment (subtask 1006) to pin the `signalapp/signal-cli` image to a specific SHA256 digest instead of `:latest`.
Review all Dockerfiles (e.g., equipment-catalog 2011, RMS 3012, finance 4013) to ensure base images are pinned to specific digests or versions, not mutable tags like `:latest`.
Integrate a container image scanning tool (e.g., Trivy, Grype) into the CI/CD `security.yml` workflow (subtask 10015).
Configure the CI/CD pipeline to block image builds or deployments if CRITICAL or HIGH severity CVEs are detected by the scanner.
Evaluate and implement the use of distroless or Alpine base images for all services to reduce the attack surface where feasible.
Consider mirroring external container images (like `signalapp/signal-cli`) to an internal registry (e.g., GHCR) after scanning.
acceptance_criteria: All container images are pinned to immutable digests or specific versions.
The CI/CD pipeline includes a container image scanning step.
Deployments are blocked if images contain CRITICAL/HIGH CVEs.
Signal-CLI image is pinned to a specific digest.
Base images are optimized for minimal attack surface (e.g., distroless/Alpine).

title: Enforce strict SQL query parameterization
description: Strengthen SQL injection protection by enforcing strict query parameterization across all services. This includes implementing lint rules or code review checklists to prohibit raw string concatenation in SQL queries and ensuring proper use of bind parameters in dynamic query builders.
subtasks: For the Rust equipment-catalog service (subtask 2004), ensure all `sqlx::QueryBuilder` filter additions exclusively use `.push_bind()` for parameters.
For the Go RMS service (subtask 3004), add a lint rule or code review checklist item that prohibits raw string concatenation in `pgx` SQL query construction.
For the Node.js social-engine service (subtask 6004), mandate the use of `postgres.js` tagged template literal syntax (`sql`...`) and prohibit string interpolation in queries.
Implement static analysis (e.g., Semgrep rules in `security.yml` 10015) to detect potential SQL injection patterns in all service codebases.
Conduct a code review focused on SQL query construction in all affected services to identify and remediate any instances of string interpolation.
acceptance_criteria: All SQL queries use parameterized statements or safe tagged template literals.
No raw string concatenation is used in SQL query construction.
Static analysis tools detect and flag potential SQL injection vulnerabilities.
Code reviews confirm adherence to strict parameterization guidelines.

title: Harden Kubernetes Infrastructure Configuration
description: Harden Kubernetes infrastructure by preventing sensitive data in ConfigMaps and enforcing Pod Security Standards. This involves implementing admission policies to reject ConfigMaps containing password patterns and applying 'restricted' Pod Security Standards to namespaces and pods.
subtasks: Implement a validating admission webhook or an OPA/Gatekeeper policy that rejects ConfigMaps containing patterns like 'password=' (subtask 1007).
Add an explicit comment in the `sigma1-infra-endpoints` ConfigMap template (subtask 1007) warning against password inclusion.
Apply the 'restricted' Pod Security Standard at the namespace level for `sigma1`, `databases`, `openclaw`, and `signal` namespaces.
Review all Deployment templates (including Signal-CLI 1006 and all services) to add `securityContext` with `runAsNonRoot: true`, `readOnlyRootFilesystem: true`, and `allowPrivilegeEscalation: false`.
Create specific Pod Security Policy exceptions only where absolutely required (e.g., for Signal-CLI 1006 if it needs elevated permissions), documenting the justification.
Verify that the `automountServiceAccountToken: false` is set for all non-ArgoCD pods (subtask 10011) to prevent unnecessary token exposure.
acceptance_criteria: ConfigMaps do not contain sensitive password information.
Admission controllers prevent deployment of ConfigMaps with password patterns.
All relevant namespaces enforce the 'restricted' Pod Security Standard.
All service pods run with `runAsNonRoot: true`, `readOnlyRootFilesystem: true`, and `allowPrivilegeEscalation: false`.
No default ServiceAccount tokens are mounted in application pods.

title: Implement human-in-the-loop for Morgan's destructive GDPR tools
description: Mitigate the risk of prompt injection in the Morgan AI agent by implementing a human-in-the-loop gate at the API level for destructive GDPR operations, specifically for the `sigma1_gdpr_delete` tool.
subtasks: Modify the `sigma1_gdpr_delete` MCP tool definition (subtask 7004) to require a signed confirmation token generated by a separate approval workflow, rather than relying solely on LLM prompt-level guards.
Implement an API endpoint (e.g., in a dedicated microservice or an existing service) that generates and validates these signed confirmation tokens.
Update the Morgan `admin.yaml` skill prompt (subtask 7002) to explicitly instruct Morgan to request human confirmation and obtain a signed token before invoking `sigma1_gdpr_delete`.
Implement rate-limiting on all `/internal/gdpr/delete` endpoints (e.g., 1 call per hour per `customer_id`) across all services.
Add an audit log check that requires a matching `sigma1_gdpr_export` call to precede any `sigma1_gdpr_delete` operation for the same `customer_id`.
Conduct a security review of Morgan's `admin` skill to ensure no other destructive actions can be taken without explicit human approval.
acceptance_criteria: `sigma1_gdpr_delete` cannot be executed without a valid, signed human confirmation token.
Destructive GDPR operations are rate-limited at the API level.
An audit log entry confirms a prior export request before a delete operation.
Morgan's `admin` skill explicitly requires human confirmation for destructive actions.
Prompt injection attempts to trigger `sigma1_gdpr_delete` without human approval are blocked.

title: Establish centralized security audit trail and alerting
description: Establish a centralized security audit trail for all security-sensitive operations and configure alerting for suspicious activities. This involves creating a dedicated audit log table, implementing structured logging across services, and setting up Loki alert rules.
subtasks: Create a dedicated audit log table in the `audit` schema for security events (e.g., `auth_failures`, `rate_limit_exceeded`, `gdpr_operations`).
Configure all services (equipment-catalog 2003/2006, finance 4010, etc.) to use structured logging (JSON) and include a `security_event` field for relevant events.
Update JWT authentication middleware (equipment-catalog 2003, finance 4010) to log authentication failures to the centralized audit log.
Update rate limit middleware (equipment-catalog 2006) to log rate limit triggers to the centralized audit log.
Configure Loki alert rules (subtask 10013) for: >10 authentication failures per minute per source IP, >5 GDPR delete operations per hour, and CiliumNetworkPolicy DROP events exceeding a baseline.
Ensure all GDPR export/delete operations (2010, 3011, 4012, 5007, 6014, 7004) log detailed information to the centralized audit table.
acceptance_criteria: A centralized audit log table exists and receives security-sensitive events.
All services use structured logging with a `security_event` field for relevant events.
Loki alerts are configured for authentication failures, rate limit triggers, and Cilium drops.
Authentication failures and rate limit events are recorded in the audit log.
GDPR operations are comprehensively logged in the audit trail.

title: Encrypt PII at rest in PostgreSQL
description: Encrypt Personally Identifiable Information (PII) stored at rest in PostgreSQL. This involves enabling storage encryption for the CloudNative-PG cluster and, for highly sensitive fields, implementing application-level encryption.
subtasks: Enable CNPG storage encryption by configuring `spec.storage.pvcTemplate` with a storage class that uses encrypted volumes for the `sigma1-postgres` cluster (subtask 1002).
Identify highly sensitive PII fields across all services (e.g., `credit_score` in vetting_results 5002, `email`/`phone` in crew_members 3003, `worker_name` in payroll_entries 4002).
For these highly sensitive fields, implement application-level encryption using `pgcrypto`'s `pgp_sym_encrypt` or integrate with a Key Management Service (KMS).
Ensure encryption keys for application-level encryption are stored in separate Kubernetes Secrets and are not directly accessible by application code.
Update database migrations (RMS 3003, Finance 4002, Customer Vetting 5002) to reflect any schema changes required for application-level encryption (e.g., changing column types to `BYTEA`).
Conduct a data audit to verify that PII in identified sensitive fields is encrypted at rest.
acceptance_criteria: PostgreSQL storage volumes are encrypted at rest.
Highly sensitive PII fields are encrypted at the application or column level.
Encryption keys are securely managed in Kubernetes Secrets or a KMS.
Data audits confirm PII is not stored in plain text.
Schema changes for encryption are correctly applied.
